text: Welcome to the robotic assisted Whipple procedure performed at Capital Health in Pennington, New Jersey.
The surgeon mobilizes the right flexure of the colon, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to facilitate access to the surgical site.
The surgeon performs extensive mobilization of the right flexure of the colon using sharp dissection and cautery, carefully separating tissue layers.
The surgeon initiates the caulker maneuver using cautery and sharp dissection techniques, focusing on precise tissue separation.
The surgeon reflects the duodenum medially, revealing underlying structures and facilitating access to the surgical site.
The kidney is visible in the background as the surgical field is exposed during the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the division of the gastrocolic ligament using energy devices, carefully separating the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the gastroduodenal junction, preparing to manipulate this anatomical area as part of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The pancreatic gland is visible on the screen, with surrounding anatomical structures partially exposed.
The surgeon identifies the gastroepiploic vessels and the gastrocolic trunk, visible within the surgical field as the duodenum is reflected medially.
The surgeon divides small attachments on the posterior part of the stomach, making the gland more prominently visible on the screen.
The gastroepiploic artery is visible on the screen, highlighted as part of the ongoing dissection near the gastroduodenal junction.
The surgeon applies double clips to a vessel near the gastroduodenal junction and then divides it.
The surgeon applies double clips to the gastroepiploic vein and divides it, with the vein clearly visible on the screen.
The surgeon navigates around the gastroduodenal junction, using umbilical tape to position and fire staples distally to the pylorus, securing the junction.
The surgeon dissects the gastroduodenal artery and divides it between clips, with visible separation of the artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon extends the dissection at the hepatic hilum, revealing the anatomical structures and vessels in this region.
Portal pain is evident in the background.
The surgeon develops a channel between the superior mesenteric vein, the portal vein, and the pancreatic gland, carefully separating these structures to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon carefully divides the head of the pancreas from the body using cautery, ensuring precise separation of the pancreatic tissue.

The surgeon completes the division of the pancreas gland and proceeds to dissect the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein from the head of the pancreas, carefully separating these vascular structures from the surrounding pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon uses a mechanical stapler to divide the intestine from the right side of the patient, ensuring precise separation of the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the small bowel mesentery, completing the separation of the space between the superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses cautery and sharp dissection to carefully separate the head of the pancreas from the body, continuing the division of the gland and developing the channel between the superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and the gland.
The surgeon divides the common bile duct just proximal to the insertion of the cystic duct, using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon completes the resection of the basement, separating it from the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the pancreatic gingivostomy, highlighting the connection between the pancreas and the gastrointestinal tract.
Proline stitches are visibly passed through the pancreatic gland and the intestine, securing these structures together.
The surgeon opens the intestine along the anti-mesenteric border, revealing the interior of the intestinal lumen.
The surgeon performs ductal mucosa anastomosis using a running suture technique with five OPDS sutures, securing the connection between the duct and surrounding tissue.
A stent is visibly placed across the anastomosis site to support the structure and prevent leaks.
The surgeon proceeds to the choledochojejunostomy, utilizing a 5-0 PDS suture to connect the bile duct to the jejunum.
we don't stand for this anastomosis
The surgeon prepares to perform the final anastomosis between the stump of the duodenum and the jejunum, using a two-layer technique similar to open surgery.
The final appearance of the completed anastomosis is shown, with the stent visibly placed across the anastomosis to support it and prevent leaks.
